title: Fitting a ventilation grille in an external cavity wall
subtitle: Cut through an external wall and fit a telescopic airbrick or louvre grille to ventilate a sub-floor void, cellar, or room
excerpt: How to fit a telescopic ventilation grille or airbrick through a cavity wall. Covers marking the opening, drilling a core or breaking out the brick, fitting the sleeve through the cavity, and fixing the inner and outer grille plates.
sourceNotes: Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). UK Building Regulations Approved Document F (ventilation requirements for sub-floor voids and habitable rooms). UK brick-laying trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A purpose-made clay, terracotta, or plastic block with open slots or holes that replaces a standard brick in an external wall to allow through-ventilation. Standard UK airbrick sizes align to standard brick courses (215 × 65 mm for a single-brick airbrick, 215 × 140 mm for a double-height). Plastic airbricks include an inner grille; terracotta airbricks are paired with a separate cavity sleeve to close off the insulated cavity and prevent cross-cavity airflow.

content.text: Fitting a ventilation grille through an external cavity wall installs an

or louvre assembly to provide continuous through-air for a sub-floor void, a cellar, or a room. The approach is cutting the opening in the outer leaf, running a sleeve through the cavity, and fitting grilles on both faces. On a solid masonry wall the job is shorter; on a cavity wall the sleeve is the critical part.

Mark the opening at brick-course height to avoid cutting through a mortar joint diagonally. Use a combination square and tape to centre the mark on a course. Check the inside face: the grille position should land on a brick or block joint internally as well. Avoid positions directly below a damp-proof course and avoid positions that will cut through a cavity tie (use a borescope or locate ties by the mortar pattern if uncertain).

For a round core-drilled grille, use a diamond core bit at 125 mm or 150 mm diameter in the SDS drill. Work from both faces to avoid a ragged breakthrough. For a rectangular airbrick, cut the mortar joints around the target brick with the angle grinder fitted with a diamond disc; chisel the brick out. Remove any cavity insulation from the opening zone (pull loose fill back or cut rigid board away flush.

Feed the telescopic or rigid sleeve through the outer hole into the cavity and then through the inner leaf. The sleeve must span the full wall thickness) typically 270 to 340 mm for a standard cavity wall. Bed the outer grille plate in a bead of silicone or mortar. Fix the inner grille plate with screws into the surrounding masonry. Do not leave the cavity open without the sleeve; an unsealed cavity lets insects and rain through.

Point around the outer grille plate with exterior mortar colour-matched to the existing joints. On the inside, fill any gap between the sleeve and the inner leaf with a fire-rated flexible filler or mortar. Once set, check the grille vents are clear and unobstructed.

attrs.symptom: Draught felt around the inner grille even with the louvre closed
attrs.cause: The sleeve is not bedded against the inner grille plate, leaving a gap around the outer edge.
attrs.fix: Remove the inner grille plate and fill the gap between sleeve and masonry with expanding foam or mortar before refitting.
